name: Recursion
explanation: A function that calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Every recursive function needs a base case (stopping condition) and a recursive case.
notes:
Recursion works by breaking a problem into smaller subproblems of the same type. The call stack holds each function call until the base case is hit, then results 'unwind' back up. Key insight: trust that the recursive call solves the smaller problem correctly, then figure out how to use that solution.

Base case examples: n==0, array is empty, node is None.
Common mistakes: forgetting the base case (infinite recursion), not moving toward the base case each call.
examples: factorial(n) = n × factorial(n-1), base: factorial(0) = 1
fibonacci(n) = fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2), base: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1
Binary search: check mid, recurse on left or right half
complexity: Time: varies (often O(n) or O(2^n)); Space: O(depth) for call stack

name: Big-O Notation
explanation: A mathematical notation describing algorithm time or space complexity relative to input size n. Describes the worst-case growth rate.
notes:
Big-O tells you how an algorithm scales as input grows — it drops constants and lower-order terms. O(1) is constant (doesn't grow), O(log n) is logarithmic (halves each step), O(n) is linear (grows with input), O(n log n) is quasilinear (merge sort), O(n²) is quadratic (nested loops), O(2^n) is exponential (brute force).

When analyzing: count the dominant operation, ignore constants. Nested loops usually mean O(n²). Halving the input each time usually means O(log n).
examples: O(1): array access by index
O(log n): binary search
O(n): linear scan / single loop
O(n log n): merge sort, heap sort
O(n²): bubble sort, selection sort, nested loops
O(2^n): recursive fibonacci without memoisation
complexity: This IS the complexity notation — used to express other complexities

name: Binary Search
explanation: An efficient algorithm that finds an element in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search range. Time complexity: O(log n). Requires the array to be sorted first.
notes:
Start with the full sorted array. Check the middle element. If it's the target, done. If target is smaller, search the left half. If larger, search the right half. Repeat until found or range is empty.

Key requirement: array MUST be sorted. Off-by-one errors are common — be careful with how you compute mid and update lo/hi.
mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2  (safer than (lo + hi) // 2 to avoid overflow)
examples: Searching for 7 in [1,3,5,7,9,11]: mid=5, 7>5, search right; mid=9, 7<9, search left; mid=7 ✓
Finding insertion point in a sorted list
Finding the first/last occurrence of a duplicate value
complexity: Time: O(log n) | Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive

name: Binary Search Tree (BST)
explanation: A binary tree where every node's left subtree contains only values smaller than the node, and the right subtree contains only larger values. Supports O(log n) operations on balanced trees.
notes:
BST property: for any node N, all nodes in N's left subtree have values < N, and all nodes in right subtree have values > N. This lets you search, insert, and delete in O(log n) average — but O(n) worst case if the tree becomes a linked list (all inserts in sorted order).

Traversals: In-order (left→node→right) gives sorted output. Pre-order (node→left→right). Post-order (left→right→node).

Deletion cases: leaf (just remove), one child (replace with child), two children (replace with in-order successor).
examples: Insert [5,3,7,1,4]: root=5, left=3, right=7, 3's left=1, 3's right=4
Search 4: go right at root? No, 4<5 go left to 3; 4>3 go right to 4 ✓
In-order traversal of above: 1,3,4,5,7
complexity: Average: O(log n) search/insert/delete | Worst: O(n) | Space: O(n)

name: AVL Tree
explanation: A self-balancing binary search tree. After every insert or delete, it checks the balance factor and performs rotations to keep height O(log n).
notes:
Balance factor = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). Must be -1, 0, or 1 at every node. If it becomes ±2, a rotation is needed.

Four rotation cases:
• Left-Left (LL): right rotation on unbalanced node
• Right-Right (RR): left rotation on unbalanced node
• Left-Right (LR): left rotate child, then right rotate node
• Right-Left (RL): right rotate child, then left rotate node

AVL trees guarantee O(log n) worst-case for all operations, unlike regular BSTs.
examples: Insert 3,2,1 into BST: becomes right-skewed. AVL detects LL imbalance, rotates right → balanced tree with root 2
Balance factor check: node with left height 3, right height 1 → BF = 2 → needs rotation
complexity: Time: O(log n) all operations, guaranteed | Space: O(n)

name: Sorting Algorithms
explanation: Algorithms that arrange elements in a specific order. Key algorithms: Bubble, Selection, Insertion (O(n²)) vs Merge, Quick, Heap (O(n log n)).
notes:
Comparison-based sorting lower bound is Ω(n log n).

Bubble Sort: repeatedly swap adjacent elements if out of order. O(n²) always.
Selection Sort: find min, place at front, repeat. O(n²) always. O(n) swaps.
Insertion Sort: build sorted portion one element at a time. O(n²) worst, O(n) best (nearly sorted). Great for small arrays.

Merge Sort: divide into halves, sort each, merge. O(n log n) always. Stable. Needs O(n) extra space.
Quick Sort: pick pivot, partition, recurse. O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst (bad pivot). In-place. Not stable.
Heap Sort: build max-heap, extract max repeatedly. O(n log n) always. In-place. Not stable.
examples: Quick sort pivot=4 on [3,1,4,1,5]: partition → [3,1,1] 4 [5] → recurse on each side
complexity: Bubble/Selection/Insertion: O(n²) | Merge/Heap: O(n log n) | Quick: O(n log n) avg

name: Graph Algorithms
explanation: Graphs are networks of nodes (vertices) and edges. Key algorithms: BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's shortest path, topological sort.
notes:
Representations: adjacency matrix (O(V²) space, O(1) edge lookup) vs adjacency list (O(V+E) space, better for sparse graphs).

BFS (Breadth-First Search): uses a queue. Explores level by level. Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs. O(V+E).
DFS (Depth-First Search): uses a stack (or recursion). Goes deep before backtracking. Used for cycle detection, topological sort. O(V+E).

Dijkstra's: shortest path in weighted graphs (non-negative weights). Uses a priority queue. O((V+E) log V).

Topological Sort: linear ordering of vertices where u comes before v for every edge u→v. Only works on DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs).
examples: BFS from A in A-B-C-D: queue=[A] → visit A, enqueue B,C → visit B, enqueue D → visit C → visit D
DFS from A: stack=[A] → visit A, push neighbours → go deep before backtracking
complexity: BFS/DFS: O(V+E) | Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) | Space: O(V+E)

name: Hash Tables
explanation: A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function. Average O(1) insert, delete, and lookup.
notes:
Hash function converts a key to an array index. A good hash function distributes keys uniformly to minimise collisions.

Collision resolution:
• Chaining: each bucket holds a linked list. O(1) average, O(n) worst.
• Open addressing: probe the next available slot (linear, quadratic, or double hashing). O(1) average if load factor is low.

Load factor α = n/m (items/buckets). Keep α < 0.75 for good performance. When exceeded, resize (rehash) — O(n) but amortised O(1).

Hash maps are behind Python dicts, Java HashMaps, JavaScript objects.
examples: hash('cat') = 3 → store 'cat' at index 3
Collision: hash('dog') = 3 too → chaining: index 3 holds ['cat', 'dog']
Load factor 0.8 → rehash: double array size, re-insert all elements
complexity: Average: O(1) insert/delete/search | Worst: O(n) | Space: O(n)

name: Dynamic Programming
explanation: An optimisation technique that solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation.
notes:
DP applies when a problem has: optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems) AND overlapping subproblems (same subproblems computed repeatedly).

Two approaches:
• Top-down (memoisation): recursion + cache. Write the recursive solution, add a dictionary to store results.
• Bottom-up (tabulation): fill a table starting from base cases, build up to the answer.

Classic problems: Fibonacci, Longest Common Subsequence, 0/1 Knapsack, Longest Increasing Subsequence, Coin Change, Edit Distance.
examples: Fibonacci with DP: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. O(n) vs O(2^n) naive.
Coin change: dp[amount] = min coins to make amount. Fill from 0 to target.
LCS: dp[i][j] = LCS of s1[:i] and s2[:j]. If chars match, +1 from dp[i-1][j-1].
complexity: Depends on problem: Fibonacci O(n), LCS O(mn), Knapsack O(nW)

name: Heaps & Priority Queues
explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree satisfying the heap property. A max-heap keeps the largest element at the root; min-heap keeps the smallest.
notes:
Heap property: in a max-heap, every parent ≥ its children. In a min-heap, every parent ≤ its children. The root is always the max (or min).

Implemented as an array: for node at index i, left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2, parent = (i-1)//2.

operations:
• Insert: add to end, bubble up (sift up). O(log n).
• Extract-max/min: remove root, replace with last element, bubble down (sift down). O(log n).
• Build heap from array: heapify all non-leaf nodes. O(n).

Priority queue: abstract data type where highest-priority element is served first. Heap is the standard implementation.
examples: Max-heap: [9,7,6,5,3,4]. Insert 8: add at end → bubble up past 6 → [9,7,8,5,3,4,6]
Extract-max from [9,7,6,5,3,4]: remove 9, put 4 at root, sift down → [7,5,6,4,3]
complexity: Insert/Extract: O(log n) | Build heap: O(n) | Space: O(n)

name: Linked Lists
explanation: A linear data structure where elements (nodes) are stored in non-contiguous memory, each pointing to the next node.
notes:
Each node holds data + a pointer to the next node. The last node points to None.

Singly linked: can only traverse forward.
Doubly linked: each node has prev and next pointers — can traverse both ways, easier deletion.
Circular: last node points back to head.

Advantages over arrays: O(1) insert/delete at known position (just update pointers). No shifting needed.
Disadvantages: O(n) search (no random access), extra memory for pointers.

Common operations: traverse, insert at head/tail/position, delete node, reverse list, detect cycle (Floyd's algorithm).
examples: Insert at head: newNode.next = head; head = newNode. O(1)
Delete node: prevNode.next = targetNode.next. O(1) with pointer to prev
Detect cycle: slow pointer moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If they meet, cycle exists.
complexity: Search: O(n) | Insert at head: O(1) | Insert at tail: O(1) with tail pointer

name: Stacks & Queues
explanation: Linear data structures with restricted access. Stack: LIFO (Last In First Out). Queue: FIFO (First In First Out).
notes:
Stack (LIFO): push adds to top, pop removes from top. Think: stack of plates. Used for: function call stack, undo operations, DFS, expression evaluation, bracket matching.

Queue (FIFO): enqueue adds to back, dequeue removes from front. Think: queue at a shop. Used for: BFS, task scheduling, print queue, producer-consumer.

Deque (double-ended queue): can insert/delete from both ends. O(1) for all operations.

Implementation: both can be implemented with arrays or linked lists. Python: list (stack), collections.deque (queue/deque). Java: Stack class, LinkedList as queue.
examples: Stack bracket matching: push '(', '['; on ')' pop and check it matches '('
BFS queue: enqueue start, dequeue node, enqueue its neighbours, repeat
Function calls: main() calls foo() calls bar() — stack: [main, foo, bar]; bar returns → [main, foo]
